title: Bisque firing schedule: cone 06
subtitle: A standard slow-climb firing cycle for low-fire bisqueware at 999°C
excerpt: Bisque firing converts bone-dry greenware into a durable, porous bisqueware ready for glazing. Cone 06 is 999°C, the standard low-fire bisque temperature. A controlled slow climb through three key temperature zones prevents cracking, burns off organic material, and completes the quartz inversion safely.
sourceNotes: Bisque firing schedule derived from first principles and standard ceramic practice. Cone temperature reference: Orton Ceramic Foundation cone chart.

attrs.body: Never open the kiln above 90°C. Ensure the kiln area is ventilated throughout the firing; organic material in clay burns off as gas between 200-400°C. Keep the kiln area clear of flammable material. Follow the kiln manufacturer's manual for initial break-in firings.
attrs.title: Kiln safety

content.text: into hard, porous bisqueware. Cone 06 (999°C) is the standard low-fire bisque temperature. The schedule below gives a slow enough climb to prevent thermal shock cracking, particularly through the critical
at 573°C. Pieces must be fully bone-dry before loading; any residual moisture will produce steam explosions inside the kiln.

measures heat work (the combination of temperature and time), not temperature alone. Cone 06 bends at approximately 999°C at a standard firing rate of 150°C per hour in the final approach. Faster climbs require a slightly higher temperature to achieve the same cone; slower climbs achieve cone bend at lower temperatures. Most electric kiln controllers accept a target temperature and ramp rate rather than a cone number directly.

Bisqueware can be stacked: rims can touch, pieces can be placed inside each other. Greenware shrinks slightly during bisque firing so pieces stacked rim-to-rim will separate as the firing progresses. Do not stack pieces with one sitting on another's base; uneven heat distribution causes cracking. Place a witness cone at mid-height in the kiln visible through the peephole.
1. Slow warm-up phase: room temperature to 120°C
Climb at 30-50°C per hour to 120°C and hold for 1 hour. This evaporates residual moisture from thick-walled pieces without steam pressure cracking. Skip this hold only for very thin, bone-dry work.

Climb at 80-100°C per hour. Between 200°C and 400°C, organic material in the clay burns off as carbon dioxide and water vapour; the kiln may produce visible fumes at this stage and the vent should remain open. By 570°C the clay is beginning to sinter and the organic content has fully burned.

Slow to 30-50°C per hour through the quartz inversion zone. The silica particles in the clay rearrange physically at 573°C and the clay expands slightly. Too fast a rise here causes cracking. This is the most critical zone in bisque firing.

Resume at 100-150°C per hour to 999°C. Hold at 999°C for 10-15 minutes to even out temperature across the kiln. Switch off and allow to cool with the lid closed; no forced cooling. The kiln can be opened cautiously once it reaches 90°C or below.

Bisqueware is hard, slightly porous, and pale; stoneware fired at cone 06 will be lighter in colour than the unfired clay because the iron content has not yet developed. The bisqueware is ready for glazing once completely cool.

items.fix: Ensure pieces are fully bone-dry before loading; use the slow warm-up schedule with a hold at 120°C
items.cause: Residual moisture in the greenware, or too fast a rise through the warm-up phase
items.symptom: Pieces crack or explode in the kiln

items.fix: Verify with a witness cone; check that the kiln controller reached 999°C at a rate slow enough to achieve full cone bend
items.cause: Kiln did not reach cone 06; temperature too low or hold too short
items.symptom: Bisqueware still porous but very fragile

items.fix: Apply kiln wash (bat wash) to shelves before each firing; bisqueware placed on bare shelves will not typically stick
items.cause: Glaze or slip dripped onto the shelf in a previous firing, or pieces placed directly on uncoated shelves
